quizTitle: Cam 17 Reading Test 01 - Fresh Passage Test

passages.title: Reading Passage 1
passages.content:
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1-13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.

The London Underground Railway

A In the early nineteenth century, many people dreamed of building an underground railway in London. The city was growing rapidly, and the streets were becoming increasingly congested. It was difficult for people to move around the city, and journey times were getting longer and longer.

B Charles Pearson was one of the first people to suggest building an underground railway in London. He believed that it would make it easier for people to travel to and from work, and would also help to reduce traffic congestion in the city centre. Pearson's idea was supported by a number of businessmen, who agreed to provide the money needed to build the railway.

C The first section of the underground railway opened in 1863, and ran from Paddington to Farringdon Street. It was an immediate success, and within a few years, more sections were added. The early trains were powered by steam engines, which produced a lot of smoke and made the tunnels very hot and dirty.

D In 1890, the first electric underground railway opened in London. This was a much cleaner and more efficient way of travelling, and electric trains soon replaced the steam engines. By the early twentieth century, the underground railway had become an essential part of London's transport system.

E Today, the London Underground is one of the busiest metro systems in the world. It carries millions of passengers every day, and serves 272 stations across the city. The underground railway has come a long way since its humble beginnings in 1863, and it continues to play a vital role in the life of the city.

questions.instruction:
Questions 1-6

Complete the notes below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.

Write your answers in boxes 1-6 on your answer sheet.
Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 7 Other countries had built underground railways before the Metropolitan line opened.

questions.instruction: Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 8 The author mentions a specific date when the first deep-level tunnel was completed.

questions.instruction: Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 9 The Metropolitan line was originally operated using steam-powered trains.

questions.instruction: Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 10 Workers building the early tunnels often suffered from poor health.

questions.instruction: Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 11 The first underground railway was built outside of London.

questions.instruction: Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 12 All of the original Metropolitan line stations are still in use today.

questions.instruction: Questions 7-13. Do the following statements agree with the information in the passage? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.
questions.content: 13 The author predicts that the cost of underground travel will continue to rise.

subQuestions.text: What is the reviewer's main purpose in the first paragraph?

options.label: A
options.text: to describe what happened during the Battle of Worcester

options.label: B
options.text: to give an account of the circumstances leading to Charles II's escape

options.label: C
options.text: to provide details of the Parliamentarians' political views

options.label: D
options.text: to compare Charles II's beliefs with those of his father

subQuestions.text: Why does the reviewer include examples of the fugitives' behaviour in the third paragraph?

options.label: A
options.text: to explain how close Charles II came to losing his life

options.label: B
options.text: to suggest that Charles II's supporters were badly prepared

options.label: C
options.text: to illustrate how the events of the six weeks are brought to life

options.label: D
options.text: to argue that certain aspects are not as well known as they should be
